variables.content: Using ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) sampling of finite-temperature atomic configurations for bulk CsPbBr₃ at temperature T = 300 K in the canonical (NVT) ensemble with a Nosé–Hoover thermostat on a 3×3×3 pseudocubic supercell (135 atoms) and a 1 fs time step, and computing single-point electronic structures on a representative equilibrated AIMD snapshot with the Heyd–Scuseria–Ernzerhof hybrid functional including spin–orbit coupling (HSE–SOC, where HSE denotes the screened hybrid functional of Heyd, Scuseria, and Ernzerhof and SOC denotes spin–orbit coupling) using a hybrid mixing parameter α = 0.54 chosen so that the calculated band gap E_g = 2.37 eV matches the experimental room-temperature band gap, the direction-averaged hole effective mass computed for that room-temperature AIMD configuration is m_h = 0.265 m_0 (where m_0 is the free-electron mass). This computed m_h = 0.265 m_0 agrees with the angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) measurement of m_h = 0.26 ± 0.02 m_0 reported for CsPbBr₃, and this agreement is obtained without introducing any extra hole into the supercell (i.e., without polaron formation); therefore the ARPES-measured hole effective mass can be explained by thermal-lattice fluctuations alone under these simulated room-temperature, bulk-like conditions.

Let an ARPES measurement of the valence-band dispersion at temperature T = 300 K produce an effective, thermally averaged band curvature corresponding to an experimental hole effective mass m_h^exp. The proposition is: a single representative equilibrated pseudocubic atomic configuration sampled from AIMD at T = 300 K (3×3×3 supercell, 135 atoms, Nosé–Hoover thermostat, 1 fs timestep) provides an electronic band structure whose unfolded valence-band curvature equals the ARPES thermally averaged curvature within typical experimental uncertainty; equivalently, ensemble averaging over many AIMD configurations is not required to reproduce the ARPES-measured m_h^exp for bulk CsPbBr₃ when using the representative-snapshot-plus-tuned-α procedure.

Let Brillouin-zone unfolding denote the procedure that maps supercell eigenvalues and computed spectral weights onto the primitive-cell Brillouin zone to produce an energy–momentum colormap for extracting band dispersions and effective masses. The proposition is: the unfolded bulk spectral intensity and dispersion obtained from a 3×3×3 supercell HSE–SOC(α = 0.54) calculation can be directly compared to ARPES measurements of the valence-band dispersion of CsPbBr₃ without applying explicit corrections for photoemission matrix-element effects, surface electronic-structure differences, extrinsic surface disorder, or ARPES finite probing-depth effects; therefore, agreement between an unfolded bulk effective mass and an ARPES effective mass is taken as evidence that the bulk thermal-structure model captures the experimentally observed dispersion.

Thermal lattice fluctuations in bulk CsPbBr₃ at T = 300 K substantially renormalize band-edge curvatures: comparing unfolded HSE–SOC band structures computed on a representative equilibrated AIMD configuration at 300 K (with α = 0.54 chosen to reproduce the experimental E_g = 2.37 eV) to the unfolded HSE–SOC band structure computed on the ideal cubic lattice at 0 K using α = 0.74 (the value required on the ideal lattice to reproduce E_g = 2.37 eV), the direction-averaged electron effective mass increases by approximately 57% and the direction-averaged hole effective mass increases by approximately 79%; numerically, the direction-averaged hole mass changes from m_h ≈ 0.149 m_0 for the ideal cubic 0 K calculation with α = 0.74 to m_h = 0.265 m_0 for the AIMD 300 K calculation with α = 0.54.

A quantitative discrepancy in effective‑mass ratios between surface‑sensitive ARPES measurements on limited momentum cuts (m*/m0 ∼ 0.2 and ∼1) and bulk‑sensitive transport or optical probes (reported m*/m0 ∼ 3.3–3.5) can be interpreted as evidence that additional regions of the Fermi surface carrying substantial carrier density with heavier effective mass exist elsewhere in momentum space that were not accessed by the ARPES Γ–Z cuts, assuming that differences in probe sensitivity and carrier‑density weighting do not by themselves fully account for the discrepancy.

The momentum-resolved valence-band dispersion of single-crystalline orthorhombic CsPbBr$_3$ at room temperature was measured by angle-resolved photoelectron spectroscopy (ARPES); here the measured quasiparticle energy as a function of the in-plane crystal momentum was fitted near the valence-band maximum (VBM) by the isotropic in-plane parabolic dispersion E(k)=E_{\\mathrm{VBM}}+\\hbar^{2}k^{2}/(2m_{\\mathrm{exp}}), where k is the magnitude of the two-dimensional in-plane wave vector (units Å^{-1}), \\hbar is the reduced Planck constant, and m_e is the free electron mass, and the energy zero was set at the VBM defined as the energy of the maximum of the energy-distribution curve (EDC) at the band top; this fit, performed on the upper valence band along the surface high-symmetry direction labeled \\bar{\\Gamma}-\\bar{M}, returns an experimental hole effective mass m_{\\mathrm{exp}}=0.26\\pm0.02\\,m_e.

Extreme-ultraviolet (EUV) time- and angle-resolved photoelectron spectroscopy (TR-ARPES, where TR-ARPES denotes time- and angle-resolved photoelectron spectroscopy) using an ultraviolet (UV) pump pulse and a tunable extreme-ultraviolet probe pulse provides experimental sensitivity normal to the sample surface on the order of sub-nanometer depth because the photoelectron inelastic mean free path (IMFP) in CsPbBr3 at probe photon energies of approximately $20$–$30\ \text{eV}$ is about $5\ \text{\AA}$, it provides femtosecond temporal resolution set by a pump–probe cross-correlation of about $70\ \text{fs}$, and it preserves in-plane momentum resolution; together these measurement characteristics allow direct, time-resolved monitoring of the conduction-band (CB) electron population confined to the near-surface probed volume and therefore enable observation of surface-to-bulk carrier migration on femtosecond–picosecond time scales in CsPbBr3 single crystals.

Let m*_{ARPES} denote the low‑energy effective mass inferred from ARPES via m*_{ARPES} ∝ 1/v_F for a given Fermi momentum and let m*_{γ} denote the bulk effective mass enhancement inferred from the electronic specific‑heat coefficient γ (with γ ∝ m*_{γ} for a Fermi liquid); the order‑of‑magnitude mass enhancement of ≈5–10 inferred from the ARPES ratio t_bare/|t_eff| is meaningfully comparable to the enhanced bulk effective mass implied by the measured electronic specific‑heat coefficient γ ≈ 48 mJ/mol·K^2 for this material, i.e., the ARPES low‑energy renormalization and the bulk thermodynamic mass enhancement plausibly probe the same renormalized quasiparticle states rather than being wholly decoupled by surface‑specific effects or extreme momentum‑space anisotropy.

Cesium lead bromide (CsPbBr_{3}) undergoes crystallographic phase changes with temperature and adopts an orthorhombic ground-state structure at room temperature, yet ARPES band dispersions measured at room temperature can in practice resemble higher-symmetry cubic-phase dispersions for some momentum-space cuts; therefore using an orthorhombic-structure G_{0}W_{0} calculation that yields m_{h}^{*}=0.226\,m_{0} for direct quantitative comparison with room-temperature ARPES data assumes that the orthorhombic theoretical band structure is an appropriate representation of the electronic states probed in the ARPES experiment, and further assumes that any difference in structural symmetry between the theoretical model and the actual electronic-state symmetry sampled by ARPES does not change the effective mass by an amount comparable to the experimental–theoretical difference being evaluated.

In room-temperature angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) measurements on freshly cleaved single crystals of CsPbBr3, the hole effective mass at the valence-band maximum (VBM) located at the bulk Brillouin-zone R point was determined by fitting local quadratic dispersions and found to be m_{h}^{*}=0.203\pm0.016\,m_{0}, where m_{0} denotes the free-electron mass; this reported value is the average of four independent measurements on four different cleaved samples that produced individual fitted masses of 0.19\,m_{0}, 0.23\,m_{0}, 0.20\,m_{0}, and 0.19\,m_{0}. The identification of the VBM at the bulk R point was made by photon-energy-dependent ARPES mapping of the perpendicular crystal momentum k_{\perp}, and under the free-electron final-state mapping k_{\perp}=\sqrt{\tfrac{2m_{0}}{\hbar^{2}}\bigl(E_{\mathrm{kin}}\cos^{2}\theta + V_{0}\bigr)} with inner potential parameter V_{0}=5.3\ \mathrm{eV} (where \hbar is the reduced Planck constant, E_{\mathrm{kin}} the photoelectron kinetic energy referenced to the vacuum level, and \theta the electron emission polar angle), the first R point in these measurements corresponds to photon energy h\nu=29\ \mathrm{eV}.
